title: How to work a knit stitch
subtitle: The single fundamental operation behind every knitted fabric.
excerpt: A knit stitch is one needle inserted, one yarn-wrap, one pull-through, one slip-off. Repeat across the row, then the next row, and a knitted piece appears.
sourceNotes:
Drawn from three public-domain references:

• Weldon's Practical Knitting (first series, 1880s), opening pages on plain knitting. Internet Archive.
• Beeton's Book of Needlework (1870), §Plain knitting. Internet Archive.
• Mary Thomas's Knitting Book (1938), §The plain stitch. (Out-of-print but widely cited as a public-domain modern reference for the technique itself.)

glossaryTerms.definition: The yarn-throwing knitting style. The working yarn is held in the right hand; the right hand wraps the yarn around the needle for each stitch.
The yarn-picking knitting style. The working yarn is held in the left hand; the right needle picks the strand for each stitch.

content.text: Knitting is built from one stitch, made over and over. The other operation people talk about (the purl stitch) is the same motion seen from the other side of the fabric. Learn the knit stitch and you can make a scarf, a dishcloth, or any flat fabric that needs no shaping.
There are two common ways to hold the yarn while knitting. The two produce the exact same finished stitch; the difference is which hand controls the yarn. Knitters call them
English style
(yarn in the right hand) and
Continental style
(yarn in the left hand). Try each for a few rows. The one you reach for without thinking is yours.
What you need

A pair of 4 mm or 5 mm knitting needles in straight or circular form, and a ball of DK or aran yarn in a smooth, light-coloured fibre. The lighter the colour the easier to see the stitches while learning. Cotton or a wool-blend feels grippier than slippery acrylic and is forgiving for first stitches.
Cast on about 20 stitches using whichever cast-on you know. The
long-tail cast on
is the standard if you're picking one fresh. Twenty stitches gives you enough to feel the row settle into a rhythm.
The knit stitch, English style
Hold the needle holding the stitches in the left hand and the empty needle in the right. The working yarn lies behind the work, held by the right hand.
Insert the tip of the right needle into the front of the first stitch on the left needle, from left to right. The needles cross with the right needle behind the left.
Wrap the working yarn around the right needle anti-clockwise from behind, bringing it between the two needles.
Pull the wrapped yarn through the loop on the left needle, so a new loop sits on the right needle.
Slip the original loop off the left needle. The new loop now lives on the right needle.
One knit stitch made. The original stitch is now on the right needle. Repeat across the row until all 20 stitches have moved from the left needle to the right.
The knit stitch, Continental style

Hold the needle holding the stitches in the left hand and the empty needle in the right, the same as in English style. The working yarn runs over the left index finger, behind the work, held under tension by the left hand.
Insert the tip of the right needle into the front of the first stitch on the left needle, from left to right.
Use the right needle to pick the working yarn from where it sits on the left index finger. The hook of the right needle should scoop the yarn from under.
Pull the picked yarn back through the loop on the left needle. A new loop now sits on the right needle.
Slip the original loop off the left needle.
Continental movements are smaller than English ones, which is why some knitters find it faster once it clicks. Stick with whichever feels natural for at least ten rows before deciding.
Turning the work and the next row

At the end of the row, swap the needles between hands so the needle now holding all the stitches sits in the left hand again. The working yarn comes from the back of the work to the back of the work; turn the piece so the yarn is now coming off the right edge of the work, behind the right needle.

Knit every stitch across this second row the same way. If you knit every row across the front and back of the work, the fabric is called garter stitch. Both sides look the same. Garter is the first-project fabric for almost every learner because there's nothing to track between rows.
Common mistakes

attrs.heading: What goes wrong on a first knit row
attrs.intro: Four things every new knitter does at least once.

items.symptom: The fabric is getting wider every row
items.cause: An extra stitch is being picked up at the start of each row, usually because the slipped first stitch and the loop hanging off the needle are being knitted as two separate stitches.
items.fix: Pull the first stitch firmly before working it. Count the stitches at the end of every row: 20 stitches cast on means 20 at the end of every row.

items.symptom: The fabric is getting narrower every row
items.cause: The last stitch of the row is dropping off the needle without being worked.
items.fix: Slow down on the last stitch. The yarn pulls firm against the edge of the work as the last stitch comes off the needle; that's the cue to check the count.

items.symptom: Stitches sit twisted on the right needle
items.cause: The yarn is being wrapped clockwise instead of anti-clockwise.
items.fix: Wrap anti-clockwise from behind. The new loop should sit on the right needle with its right leg in front of the needle.

items.symptom: The work twists around itself before row two
items.cause: The cast-on row got twisted when the stitches were transferred to the left hand to begin row one.
items.fix: There's no fix once a few rows are in. Undo back to the cast-on, lay the cast-on row flat with all loops facing the same direction, and start row one again. Worth checking before the second row settles.

content.text: Where this goes next

Once the knit stitch feels automatic, the natural next thing is the purl stitch (the same operation worked from the other side of the fabric). With the two stitches in hand, every other knitted fabric becomes available: stocking stitch is knit one row and purl the next; rib alternates the two across the same row; cables and lace are the two stitches in different orders.
